pattern_recognition: Backtracking - The problem can be categorized under backtracking as we need to explore all possible combinations of elements in the array to form subsets. The signals for this pattern include the need to explore multiple paths and the generation of combinations.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible combinations of elements by using binary representation of numbers from 0 to 2^n - 1, where n is the length of the input array.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through all integers from 0 to 2^n - 1. For each integer, use its binary representation to determine which elements to include in the current subset.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use backtracking to build subsets incrementally. This approach avoids generating all combinations upfront and only constructs valid subsets during the recursive calls.
approaches.algorithm: Define a recursive function that takes the current index and the current subset. At each step, decide to either include or exclude the current element and recursively call the function for the next index.

dry_run: Consider the input array nums = [1, 2]. Start with an empty subset. At index 0, include 1 to form [1] and exclude it to form []. Move to index 1, include 2 to form [1, 2] and exclude it to form [1]. Backtrack to index 0 and exclude 1 to form [2] and then exclude 2 to form []. The final subsets are [], [1], [2], [1, 2].
edge_cases: An empty input array should return a single empty subset. A single element array should return two subsets: the empty subset and the subset containing the element. Duplicates are not applicable here as the input is guaranteed to be unique. Large input sizes may lead to performance issues due to exponential growth in the number of subsets.
common_pitfalls: 1. Forgetting to include the empty subset in the output. 2. Incorrectly handling the index during recursion, leading to missing subsets. 3. Not considering the unique constraint of the input array, which may lead to incorrect assumptions about duplicate handling.
